Your direct report told you about the divorce during the 1:1. You held space. You listened. You got home and couldn't find space for yourself.
Your VP's direction is wrong and everyone knows it. You're the only one who hasn't said it yet. The silence is getting louder.
You got promoted and the emptiness came with you. The title you wanted didn't fill the space you thought it would.

Your team got restructured and the people scattered. The network you spent two years building is now three different companies.
You're responsible for other people while hiding your own doubt about whether you're qualified for this.

You're responsible for other people's feelings while hiding your own, which makes social anxiety feel like a system failure.

Your chest tightens calculating if you can buy the supplies your classroom needs. You'll buy them anyway because students need them.
A student you loved is gone and the school moves forward. Your chest feels hollow knowing nobody's assigned you time to grieve.
A veteran teacher stops by to watch her lesson. Her throat closes. She knows the content. Her body doesn't believe she's allowed to know it.
She's three hours home from school and the lesson she taught is still running in her head. The moment the instruction didn't land keeps replaying at full volume.
Your chest tightens when their test score becomes your worth. You teach a human and you're measured by a number.
It's Sunday at 10pm. She's not tired. Her body is already running Monday's schedule — attendance, the IEP at noon, the student who needs to be separated from his seat partner.

Diane woke at 3:14am with her heart already ahead of her, cataloging everything that could go wrong before she'd opened her eyes.
Her mother calls every morning at 8am and Diane has been answering for fifteen years, even on the mornings she has a 9am meeting and the call always runs forty minutes.
She scheduled her mother's cardiologist, her son's IEP meeting, her own mammogram, and the quarterly review all in the same week, and that was a normal week.
Her mother called in crisis, her daughter called in crisis, and a colleague needed support, and Diane answered all three calls and then sat in her car unable to feel anything at all.
At forty-nine, Diane said the thing in the marriage that she had been not-saying for eleven years, and the marriage survived, and she has not stopped thinking about what else she has been not-saying.
She was fine at work, fine at dinner, fine at every event anyone could see — it was only alone, in the car, in the shower, that she noticed she hadn't looked forward to anything in months.

Her mother's memory care costs $6,400 a month and Diane's salary is $87,000 a year and she is doing the math in her head during every meeting.
Her mother looked at her last Tuesday and didn't know her name, and Diane drove home and cooked dinner and helped with homework and didn't cry until 11pm because that was the only time available.
She has twenty-two years of experience and a graduate degree and she still lowers her voice slightly when she speaks in the Monday meeting where all the men are older and louder.
She has been planning for every contingency for so long that she can no longer tell the difference between strategic thinking and anxiety looking for a container.
She could list what she'd done for everyone else this year with precision and couldn't answer when her therapist asked what she'd done for herself.
She wakes at 3:17am and the night sweats have already passed and now it's the thoughts, arriving with the same precision they showed up the night before and the night before that.
She used to be the person who could work any room; now she sits in the parking lot of the party for ten minutes before deciding whether to go in.
Her shoulders have been at her ears for so long she forgot that was a choice her body was making.
